stem: \( f(x) \) has derivative \( f'(x)=x^{2}-([[s]])x+([[p]]) \). At which \( x \) does \( f \) have a local extremum? (Give one.)
solution: \( f'(x)=0 \) at \( x=[[r1]] \) and \( x=[[r2]] \); one extremum is at \( x=[[answer]] \).

stem: Suppose \( f'(x) = x^{2} - ([[s]])x + ([[p]]) \) for all real \( x \). Because this derivative is an upward-opening parabola, \( f \) is increasing, then decreasing, then increasing again as \( x \) passes through its two roots. At which \( x \)-value does \( f \) attain a LOCAL MINIMUM?
solution: Since \( f'(x)=(x-[[r1]])(x-[[r2]]) \) has positive leading coefficient, \( f \) decreases on \( ([[r1]],[[r2]]) \) and increases outside it, so the local minimum is at the larger root, \( x=\max([[r1]],[[r2]])=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the local MAXIMUM (the smaller root), not the minimum.
This has the wrong sign on the larger root.
This computes \( s-p \), not a root of \( f' \).
This is the smaller root \( r_1 \), which is the local maximum.
This is off by one from the correct root.
This is off by one from the correct root.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{3} + ([[b]])x^{2} + ([[c]])x + ([[d]]) \). \( f \) has a critical point at \( x=[[x0]] \), and this critical point is a local extremum. What is the value of that local extremum, \( f([[x0]]) \)?
solution: The extreme value is simply \( f \) evaluated at the critical point: \( f([[x0]]) = [[a]]([[x0]])^{3}+([[b]])([[x0]])^{2}+([[c]])([[x0]])+([[d]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( f'([[x0]]) \), which equals 0 by construction, not \( f([[x0]]) \).
This drops the constant term \( d \).
This reports only the constant term instead of evaluating \( f \) at \( [[x0]] \).
This uses the wrong (unrelated) formula.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Suppose \( f'(x) = x^{2} - ([[s]])x + ([[p]]) \) for all real \( x \), so \( f \) has critical points at \( x=[[r1]] \) and \( x=[[r2]] \). Using the second derivative test, what is \( f''(x) \) evaluated at the LOCAL MAXIMUM of \( f \)?
solution: \( f''(x) = 2x - ([[s]]) \). Since \( f'(x)=(x-[[r1]])(x-[[r2]]) \), the local maximum is at the smaller root \( x=[[r1]] \), and \( f''([[r1]]) = 2([[r1]]) - ([[s]]) = [[answer]] \), which is negative, confirming a local maximum.
distractor_rationales: This is \( f''(x) \) at the local MINIMUM \( r_2 \), not the maximum.
This has the wrong sign (always non-negative), the mirror image of the local-min value.
This reports \( s \) rather than \( f''(r_1) \).
This adds instead of subtracts \( s \).
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: A ball's height in meters, \( t \) seconds after launch, is \( h(t) = -[[a]]t^{2} + [[b]]t + [[h0]] \). At what time \( t \) does the ball reach its maximum height?
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 2 in the denominator.
This inverts the ratio of coefficients.
This reports the initial height, not the time.
This reports twice the leading coefficient, not a time.
This is off by one from the correct time.
This is off by one from the correct time.

stem: Suppose \( f'(x) = (x-([[r1]]))(x-([[r2]]))(x-([[r3]])) \) for all real \( x \). This cubic derivative has three distinct real roots, and its leading coefficient is positive. Of the three critical points of \( f \), at which \( x \)-value does \( f \) attain its LOCAL MAXIMUM (as opposed to one of its two local minima)?
solution: For a cubic \( f' \) with positive leading coefficient and roots \( [[r1]] < [[r2]] < [[r3]] \), \( f \) decreases, then increases, then decreases, then increases across the roots, so the outer roots \( [[r1]] \) and \( [[r3]] \) are local minima and the middle root \( [[r2]] \) is the local maximum. So the answer is \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the smaller outer root, a local minimum, not the maximum.
This is the larger outer root, a local minimum, not the maximum.
This averages the two outer roots instead of picking the middle root.
This is off by one from the correct root.
This is off by one from the correct root.
This is off by two from the correct root.
